claim: In Albrecht's 1999 informal study of 254 dog owners, mixed-breed dogs traveled an average of 14 miles before pickup while purebred dogs traveled only 2 miles on average — a 7x difference likely driven by public willingness to rescue perceived 'valuable' dogs sooner.
stance_target: Breed/type significantly affects effective search radius and recovery strategy
scope_conditions: Informal study, n=254; data from 1999; USA context; should be treated as directional, not definitive
quoted_support: Mixed-breed dogs were allowed to travel an average of 14 miles before they were picked up, whereas purebred dogs were allowed to travel only an average of 2 miles before they were picked up.
numbers: 14 miles average mixed-breed travel before pickup
2 miles average purebred travel before pickup

claim: Toy breeds were the least likely to travel more than 0.75 miles from where last seen; pointing breeds traveled farther than retrieving breeds.
stance_target: Breed-specific search radius calibration is necessary for efficient field operations
scope_conditions: Albrecht 1999 informal study; directional guidance for small-dog and pointing-breed cases
quoted_support: Toy breeds were the least likely to travel more than three-quarters of a mile from where they were last seen. Breed and size seemed to have an effect on the distance traveled—pointing breeds were more likely to travel farther than retrieving breeds.

claim: Lord et al. 2007 found 71% of 187 lost dogs were recovered; 29% were never found — and the research questions why improved behavioral knowledge and search techniques could not increase this rate.
stance_target: Evidence-based search protocols could significantly improve the 71% baseline recovery rate
scope_conditions: Lord et al. 2007 study; n=187 lost dogs; data collected 2005
quoted_support: The study determined that of the 187 dogs that were lost, 132 (71%) of them were recovered. But when I read the study, my first question was this: What happened to the other 29% of lost dogs, and why were they never found?

claim: Geographic barriers — canyons, freeway walls, raging rivers — act as hard direction-of-flight modulators and directly constrain probability zones in search planning.
stance_target: Terrain mapping is a required input to probability-based search area delineation
scope_conditions: General lost dog search planning; specifically relevant to Algarve terrain (cliffs, river valleys, motorways)
quoted_support: a canyon, a brick wall along a freeway, and a raging river are all geographical barriers that would inhibit and influence a dog's direction of flight.

claim: Population density at the escape point inversely affects travel distance: a dog lost in dense urban Manhattan is likely recovered quickly by a passerby; a dog lost at an isolated rest stop may travel 30+ miles.
stance_target: Search radius must be calibrated to population density, not just breed/temperament
scope_conditions: All lost dog scenarios; particularly relevant for low-density rural Algarve vs. urban Faro/Portimão contexts
quoted_support: A dog that slips their collar while being walked in midday in Manhattan... is going to travel much less of a distance compared to a dog that slips their collar at a rest stop in an isolated area in the middle of Nevada.
numbers: 30-mile radius for isolated highway rest stop case

claim: Skittish fearful dogs that escape near isolated areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks) tend to remain in those quiet areas and establish a 'residence' rather than continuing to travel.
stance_target: Quiet refuge identification is a key search strategy for skittish dog profiles after day 3-4
scope_conditions: Skittish/fearful dogs; applies to Algarve rural terrain with patches of woodland, cork forest, and isolated agricultural land
quoted_support: many dogs with fearful temperaments that escape near isolated areas (wooded areas, golf courses, cemeteries, large parks, etc.) prefer to remain in those quiet areas.

claim: Monetary reward offers likely activate the brain's 'pleasure center' (nucleus accumbens), suppressing the altruistic motivation (posterior superior temporal sulcus) — meaning reward-motivated searchers are less likely to behave cooperatively and more likely to chase.
stance_target: Framing lost dog posters with 'PLEASE HELP FIND' rather than 'REWARD OFFERED' produces better searcher behavior
scope_conditions: Based on neuroscience research cited in 'Sway: The Irresistible Pull of Irrational Behavior'
quoted_support: the financial compensation part of our brain simply cannot operate at the same time as the altruistic part of the brain... the nucleus accumbens is a primitive part of the brain that is known as the 'pleasure center'... it makes more sense to appeal to the altruism center and advise dog owners to use the words 'PLEASE HELP FIND' on their posters rather than 'REWARD OFFERED.'
